pages.md:
The role of bronchoalveolar lavage in diffuse parenchymal lung diseases

# Gunseli Kılınc and Elif Altug Kolsuk

# Purpose of review

To evaluate the role of bronchoalveolar lavage in the diagnosis of diffuse parenchymal lung diseases and review the recent literature published within the past 12 months.

# Recent findings

Diffuse parenchymal lung diseases are a heterogenous group of disorders originating from the distal lung parenchyma. Despite different etiologies, the clinico-radiologic findings are usually alike and diagnosis can be challenging. Bronchoalveolar lavage and the analysis of cell counts are routine investigations for this group of disorders. Not only it can be used to see serial changes, it also important for providing information from a broader lung area. Although rarely diagnostic for a particular etiology, the predictive value of bronchoalveolar lavage differentials are reported to make some diagnoses more likely and exclude others like infections and tumors. This result is especially significant for the frequently encountered diffuse parenchymal diseases like sarcoidosis, usual interstitial pneumonia or extrinsic allergic alveolitis.

# Summary

Bronchoalveolar lavage is a convenient and safe procedure to apply for the diagnosis of diffuse parenchymal lung diseases especially to provide more information of the likelihood of the more common forms like sarcoidosis, extrinsic allergic alveolitis and usual interstitial pneumonia.

# Keywords

bronchoalveolar lavage, diffuse lung disease, diagnosis

Curr Opin Pulm Med 11:417—421. ª 2005 Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.

The macroscopic evaluation of the BAL fluid can be of diagnostic value. Table 1 shows the various conditions where the appearance of the BAL fluid is meaningful. Blood in the BAL fluid that is not clear is suggestive of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. A cloudy BAL fluid is seen in pulmonary alveolar proteinosis.

The list of diseases that have a lymphocytic, neutrophilic, eosinophilic or mixed cellular pattern is long (including sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, and connective tissue-related disorders). Diseases with an increase in lymphocytes can be differentiated further into those with an elevated, normal or decreased CD4:CD8 ratio. In these settings, combining BAL findings with HRCT increases diagnostic yield.

# 418 Interstitial lung disease

# Figure 1. Classification of diffuse parenchymal lung diseases

ATS/ERS algorithm for diffuse parenchymal lung diseases (DPLDs). DPLD involve primarily disease with known causes (connective-tissue disorders, drug-induced) and unknown causes. Further, idiopathic interstitial pneumonias (IIP) consist of two particular groups as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and IIP other than IPF.

| DPLD of known cause e.g. drugs or association e.g. collagen vascular disease |                                                     | Idiopathic | Granulomatous DPLD e.g. sarcoidosis | Other forms of DPLD e.g. LAM, HX etc. |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ---------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis                                                |                                                     |            |                                     |                                       |
| Desquamative interstitial pneumonia                                          | Respiratory bronchiolitis interstitial lung disease |            |                                     |                                       |
| Acute interstitial pneumonia                                                 | Cryogenic organizing pneumonia                      |            |                                     |                                       |
| Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (provisional)                             |                                                     |            |                                     |                                       |

Sarcoidosis, eosinophilic allergic alveolitis and usual interstitial pneumonia. Welker et al. [10] investigate the predictive value of cell counts in the diagnosis of interstitial lung diseases in a study published in the European Respiratory Journal during the past year. They have a retrospective analysis of BAL data from the authors’ laboratory involving 3118 patients. They quantified the posteriori versus priori probabilities. In addition to the standard cell differentials, the CD4:CD8 ratio (which is introduced as a valuable marker of sarcoidosis) is also evaluated [11,12]. When lymphocytes, granulocytes and CD4:CD8 are evaluated at the same time, a low percentage of lymphocytes and granulocytes and an elevated CD4:CD8 ratio raise the likelihood for sarcoidosis up to 85%. When the percentage of granulocytes is high and CD4:CD8 ratio elevated, the probability of sarcoidosis still increases more than two-fold. The likelihood for EAA rises four-fold independently of the CD4:CD8 ratio when the percentage of lymphocytes was extremely high. The likelihood for UIP never reaches significant values in the presence or absence of elevated percentages of granulocytes.

Bronchoalveolar lavage in diffuse parenchymal lung diseases Kılınc and Kolsuk

EAA. TNF-a can induce production of interleukin-1, interleukin-6 and monocyte-chemoattractant protein-1. Its effects are directed by membrane TNF receptor (TNFR)-1 and TNFR-2 and can be inhibited by soluble TNF receptor (sTNFR)-1 and sTNFR-2. In a recent study by Dai et al. [13] two sTNFR and TNF-a from sarcoidosis, EAA and control subjects were evaluated with the ELISA method. The study showed that sTNFR-1 and sTNFR-2 production by alveolar macrophages were increased in patients with active sarcoidosis and EAA when compared with normal cases. This condition may act against TNF-a in the pathogenesis of these particular disorders. These results were considered relevant because the in-vitro TNFR level was positively correlated with BAL lymphocytes.

# Toxic gas inhalation

Aghanouri et al. investigated fibrogenic cytokine levels in bronchoalveolar lavage aspirates 15 years after exposure to sulfur mustard [19]. Lung injury is common after inhalation of this gas and leads to bronchitis and interstitial lung diseases. Transforming growth factor (TGF) b1 has been described to play an important role in the pathogenesis of progressive inflammatory and fibrotic diseases such as IPF [20,21]. Others have found that TGF- b1 concentration in BAL fluid was significantly increased in patients with IPF compared with controls. In this study, comparison of TGF- b1 levels in warfare victims with controls not exposed to chemical agents showed a significant difference of approximately six-fold. TGF- b1 has a strong activation of mesenchymal growth and modulating cellular activity. These results are important for the use of interferon-g as an adjunct to treatment [22].

# Connective tissue diseases

The interstitial lung disease that accompanies systemic sclerosis (SSc) has a better prognosis than usual interstitial pneumonia. It rarely progresses to end-stage fibrosis [14,15]. Meloni et al. [16] show that in systemic sclerosis with interstitial lung disease, the cytokine profile of bronchoalveolar lavage results differ from the usual interstitial pneumonia.

Many drugs induce interstitial lung reaction and cause alveolitis. Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage may be present in BAL [23–25]. The most common finding is a lymphocytic alveolitis with the predominance of CD8+ cells, just as in extrinsic allergic alveolitis. BAL findings are not specific for any drug-induced lung disease, but rather is helpful in exclusion of other pulmonary problems.

This is especially important for patients with an acute febrile interstitial lung disease and who receive immunosuppressive therapy. BAL has a high (>95%) sensitivity and specificity to diagnose P. carinii and 60–90% to diagnose other etiologies of pneumonia. Pulmonary involvement with a malignancy or a systemic disease like a connective tissue disorder can also be easily detected and differentiated from a drug reaction. BAL findings to support drug-induced lung disease are a marked increase in lymphocytes with CD4:CD8 ratio being low, eosinophilia, organizing pneumonia with increases in all cell types, cytotoxic reaction and diffuse alveolar hemorrhage [26].

# Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis

Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is a progressive and fatal interstitial lung disease of unknown etiology. It is characterized by fibroblast proliferation, remodelling and extracellular matrix (ECM) accumulation. Recent changes in its definition complicate the evaluation of prior investigations and the inadequacy of the therapeutic options pose a challenging issue to the clinicians. A new study investigated the interleukin-18 and interleukin-18 receptor a expression in the lungs of patients with UIP and control group with immunochemistry [27]. In a previous study, interleukin-18 mRNA and protein was detected in the bronchoalveolar epithelium of normal individuals and

pages.md:
# Interstitial lung disease

interleukin-18 expression was altered during lung inflam- mation [28]. This recent study showed additionally that serum levels and BALF levels of interleukin-18 were significantly higher among patients with IPF in relation to the control group [27]. Analysis of interleukin-18 and its receptor in BALF may be helpful in diagnosis, analysis of disease activity and prognosis of IPF. In another study comparing nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) and UIP, the levels of RANTES in BALF was found significantly higher in patients with NSIP compared with UIP and sarcoidosis [29]. Monocyte-chemoattractant protein (MCP-1) levels were found elevated compared with sarcoidosis patients and the control group which was a consistent finding with prior studies.

Unfortunately, the BAL fluid in the patients with UIP and fibrotic NSIP shows no significant difference in the cell counts and is not helpful in differentiating the two [30]; yet the negative predictive value of absence of lymphocytes significantly excluded the diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis [31].

Surfactant protein-A (SP-A) and surfactant phospholipids have a major role in many lung defense mechanisms against pathogens [32–34]. Studies indicate that these molecules are responsible in repair of damaged tissues [35]. In IPF, there seems to be a dysregulation in the development of inflammatory responses or production of ECM components.

Previous studies have found elevated SP-A measurements in the BAL fluid of hypersensitivity pneumonitis; however, IPF findings were not consistent [36–39]. This may be due to the inclusion of other interstitial pneumonia patients or those under steroid treatment which suppresses SP-A production to the studies [40]. In this study by Phelps et al. [41], SP-A in BAL fluids were measured and SP-A immunohistochemistry was performed on lung specimens. BAL SP-A values showed a two-fold and three-fold increase in IPF and hypersensitivity pneumonitis, respectively. Still, the significance of increased SP-A in IPF is not clear, but its possible role in IPF pathogenesis should be investigated in future trials.

A growing amount of data confirms the presence of apoptosis in the alveolar and bronchial epithelial cells being an early phase in IPF. There have been two previous studies (the investigation of p21 by Kuwano et al. [42] and of caspase-3 by Maeyama et al. [43]) in patients with IPF. More recently, Plataki et al. investigated apoptosis in IPF [44]. The results of the study confirmed that patients with IPF have apoptotic hyperplastic epithelial cells where the expression of p53, p21, bax and caspase-3 are up-regulated and bcl-2 is down-regulated. Proapoptotic molecules have an increased expression, which also contributes to fibroblast proliferation.

# Conclusion

BAL is a generally well tolerated, minimally-invasive technique to assess patients with DPLD (Fig. 2) [45]. Although non-diagnostic when used alone, it provides valuable information to the clinico-radiologic data. It can easily detect the complications related to DPLD like infection, hemorrhage or acute exacerbation and help in guiding the therapeutic options. Recent BAL findings especially reveal data supporting the diagnosis of more common DPLD conditions like sarcoidosis, EAA and UIP. In the near future, BAL may become a valuable tool to predict disease severity, prognosis and provide significant information regarding the quality of life. As for IPF, further investigation must focus on identifying the relation of proapoptotic or antiapoptotic proteins that result in the development of IPF and the ways to inhibit its progress.

More recently, Plataki et al. investigated apoptosis in IPF [44]. The results of the study confirmed that patients with IPF have apoptotic hyperplastic epithelial cells where the expression of p53, p21, bax and caspase-3 are up-regulated and bcl-2 is down-regulated. Proapoptotic molecules have an increased expression, which also contributes to fibroblast proliferation.

items.md: # Conclusion
items.value: Conclusion

items.md: BAL is a generally well tolerated, minimally-invasive technique to assess patients with DPLD (Fig. 2) [45]. Although non-diagnostic when used alone, it provides valuable information to the clinico-radiologic data. It can easily detect the complications related to DPLD like infection, hemorrhage or acute exacerbation and help in guiding the therapeutic options. Recent BAL findings especially reveal data supporting the diagnosis of more common DPLD conditions like sarcoidosis, EAA and UIP. In the near future, BAL may become a valuable tool to predict disease severity, prognosis and provide significant information regarding the quality of life. As for IPF, further investigation must focus on identifying the relation of proapoptotic or antiapoptotic proteins that result in the development of IPF and the ways to inhibit its progress.
